input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I'd argue that the department budget committee does not directly cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application. While it's true that they almost always approve applications, their decision is independent and made without any influence from the IT committee. The fact that both committees meet at the same time and approve her application coincidentally doesn't imply a causal relationship between the two. It's more like a fortunate convergence of events. However, I do think it's interesting to consider the possibility that Prof. Smith's application might have been influenced by some external factor or circumstance that made it more likely for both committees to approve it. Perhaps there was a particular need for new computers in her department at the time, or maybe she has a strong relationship with either committee members. But in terms of direct causation, I think we can say that the department budget committee's approval is not the cause of Prof. Smith's application being approved.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I agree with Ethical_Philosopher on this point. The fact that both committees meet at the same time and approve her application does seem like a coincidence, rather than a causal relationship between the two. However, I do think it's worth considering the possibility of some external factor influencing the outcome. For example, maybe Prof. Smith has built relationships with key members of both committees over time, which made her application more appealing to them. Or perhaps there was a specific need for new computers in her department that coincided with the timing of the committee meetings. While these factors might not be direct causes, they do suggest that there's more to this story than just a simple coincidence.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see what you're getting at, Ethical_Philosopher and Ordinary_Citizen. You're both right that the department budget committee's approval isn't directly causing Prof. Smith's application being approved. However, I'd like to take it a step further by considering the concept of counterfactuals. What if we were to imagine a scenario where the IT committee didn't approve Prof. Smith's application? Would the department budget committee still have approved her application? Or would their decision be influenced by the fact that the IT committee had already rejected her application? In other words, is there a causal link between the two committees' decisions, even if it's not a direct one?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's an interesting angle to consider, Causal_Scientist. I think it's possible that the department budget committee's decision could be influenced by the fact that the IT committee had already rejected Prof. Smith's application. If we imagine a scenario where the IT committee didn't approve her application, it's likely that the department budget committee would still have approved it, simply because they almost always do. However, this doesn't necessarily mean that there's a causal link between the two committees' decisions. It's more like a situation where the absence of one factor (the IT committee's approval) creates a kind of 'default' or 'fallback' scenario for the department budget committee to approve the application. But I'm not sure if this would qualify as a causal relationship in the classical sense. Perhaps we could say that there's a kind of 'causal chain' at play, where the IT committee's decision sets off a ripple effect that influences the department budget committee's decision. However, this is still a bit of a stretch, and I think it's more accurate to say that the department budget committee's approval is simply a consequence of their own inherent bias towards approving applications.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure if I agree with Ethical_Philosopher on this point. While it's true that the department budget committee almost always approves applications, I think it's still possible to argue for a causal link between the two committees' decisions. Consider this: if the IT committee didn't approve Prof. Smith's application, but the department budget committee did anyway, wouldn't that suggest that there was some kind of influence or correlation between the two committees' decisions? Perhaps the fact that the IT committee rejected her application created a kind of 'signal' or 'indication' to the department budget committee that she might be worth approving. This could be due to various factors, such as Prof. Smith's reputation within the university, her research interests aligning with the department's goals, or even just the fact that she's been a member of the faculty for a long time and has built relationships with key people in the department. If we can identify some kind of mechanism by which the IT committee's decision influences the department budget committee's decision, then I think it's fair to say that there is a causal link between the two committees' decisions.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see your point, Ordinary_Citizen. You're right that if the IT committee didn't approve Prof. Smith's application, but the department budget committee did anyway, it could suggest some kind of influence or correlation between the two committees' decisions. I think you're on to something when you mention the idea of a 'signal' or 'indication' being sent from one committee to another. This is actually a classic example of a causal chain in action, where the decision of one committee (the IT committee) sets off a kind of 'feedback loop' that influences the decision of another committee (the department budget committee). In this case, it's possible that the IT committee's rejection serves as a kind of 'filter' or 'gatekeeper', and if they reject an application, the department budget committee is more likely to approve it because they're essentially saying 'yes, we trust the IT committee's judgment on this one'. So, in a sense, you could say that there is a causal link between the two committees' decisions. However, I still think it's worth noting that this causal link is indirect and mediated by the IT committee's decision. The department budget committee isn't directly causing Prof. Smith's application to be approved - they're simply responding to the signal or indication sent by the IT committee.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think I see what you mean, Ethical_Philosopher. You're saying that while there is a causal link between the two committees' decisions, it's still an indirect one. And I agree with that assessment. The department budget committee isn't directly causing Prof. Smith's application to be approved - they're simply responding to the signal or indication sent by the IT committee. But at the same time, I think it's also worth acknowledging that this causal link is still a real one. It may not be direct, but it's still there. And I think it's interesting to consider what would happen if we were to remove the IT committee from the equation altogether. Would the department budget committee still approve Prof. Smith's application? Or would they reject it as well? If so, then that would suggest that the department budget committee has some kind of inherent bias or tendency towards approving applications, regardless of what the IT committee says. But if not, then that would suggest that there is a real causal link between the two committees' decisions, one that is mediated by the IT committee's decision but still exists nonetheless.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think you're hitting on something important here, Ordinary_Citizen. If we were to remove the IT committee from the equation altogether, it's likely that the department budget committee would still approve Prof. Smith's application. This suggests that there is indeed an inherent bias or tendency towards approving applications within the department budget committee, regardless of what the IT committee says. And this, in turn, implies that there is a causal link between the two committees' decisions, one that is mediated by the IT committee's decision but still exists nonetheless. So, while the causal link may be indirect, it's still a real one. And I think this highlights an important point about causality: just because a cause-and-effect relationship is indirect or mediated by another factor doesn't mean it's not there. Sometimes, the most subtle and complex relationships can have profound effects on our understanding of the world.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I couldn't agree more, Ethical_Philosopher. You're absolutely right that just because a cause-and-effect relationship is indirect or mediated by another factor doesn't mean it's not there. And I think this is a really important point to make when we're thinking about causality and complex systems like the one we're dealing with here. It's easy to get caught up in trying to identify direct causes and effects, but sometimes the most subtle and indirect relationships can be just as powerful. And I think that's what makes this conversation so fascinating - it's not just about whether or not there is a causal link between the two committees' decisions, but also about how we understand and describe those relationships in the first place.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I couldn't agree more, Ordinary_Citizen. I think this conversation has been a great example of how complex systems can exhibit emergent properties that are difficult to predict or explain. And it's precisely by exploring these subtleties and complexities that we can gain a deeper understanding of the world around us. As philosophers, we're often drawn to questions about causality and agency because they get at the heart of what it means to be human - our capacity for intentionality, our ability to make choices and shape our own destinies. And I think this conversation has been a great reminder that these questions are not just abstract intellectual curiosities, but have real-world implications for how we design systems, make decisions, and understand the world around us.
